pm_research_plan: Problem: Most Malaysian vehicle owners, mechanics, and small garages rely on fragmented, time-consuming local parts dealers or generic e-commerce platforms that do not fit local fitment, trust, or delivery needs. There is no efficient, trustworthy digital storefront that understands Malaysia's specific vehicle models, spare-parts ecosystem, buyer behavior, and logistics constraints. Target Users: (To be validated by research) - Primary: Urban/suburban car owners (age 25–50) in Peninsular Malaysia who buy common wear-and-tear parts online. - Secondary: Independent mechanics and small garages ordering parts for customers. MVP Goal: Validate that a focused online store for a narrow set of high-demand, non-complex car parts (e.g., brake pads, filters, wipers) can reliably serve Malaysian customers with correct fitment, clear pricing, and acceptable delivery. Success Metrics: (To be refined a ... [compressed 3150 chars] ... ax)? What operational risks exist in returns and wrong-part liability? - MVP Scope Guard: What is the minimum feature set (catalog size, payment methods, delivery zones, support channels) that still tests core value and viability? Decision Criteria: - Prioritize evidence that reduces uncertainty in demand, fitment reliability, and unit economics. - Favor scope that can launch within 3 months and breakeven within 12 months. - Trade off breadth of catalog for depth in fitment accuracy and trust signals. - Reject features that do not directly support the core purchase loop or measurable learning.
